item.title: Update 09 '24
item.description: September is here, it’s getting darker on my way home from work earlier and earlier. I had a good summer, stayed by Lake Garda in a small commune for a while. Went to Paris for a couple days but mostly stayed in London and hung out. When the seasons change it’s also the most ideal time to make any changes in your life. I’m trying to reduce my screen time, cut out Instagram (successful so far) and remove reliance on my phone in general - I find that I listen to the same music over and over again and want to rediscover finding new music.

item.title: Update 06 '24
item.description: It’s now six months into the year so I thought I’d write a post about where I’m at, what I’ve been doing. My girlfriend and I went travelling to Korea and Japan, starting off from Seoul -> Jeju -> Busan -> Kyoto -> Tokyo. It was a trip I had been planning for a while, we both had quit our jobs and with some money saved it was a good time to do it.

item.title: some books I read in 2023 w/ notes
item.description: I didn’t get around to reading that much this year at all but managed a few. It was personally a transitional and exciting year with travelling and career change so I suppose reading wasn’t on my mind as I was very up in the air but hoping this year I float down to the ground again. This Is How You Lose the Time War - Amal El-Mohtar and Max Gladstone Read this while travelling around Korea - beautiful sci-fi romance, very poetic and deeply moving.

item.title: VR Waiting Room
item.description: I dig into the earth with my fingers, the soil grips and feeds its way into my pores and takes shelter underneath my nails. I know this isn’t real. The soil is soaked with rain water, roots uprooted and micro insects flee from the disruption I’ve caused to their universe. I know that this is not real but it’s so peaceful, there is no sound but the occasional bird song. The clouds parted above me to reveal a flight departure screen hovering above, its opacity changing so that it wouldn’t be a distraction to the experience unless you were directly looking at it to check your flight.

item.title: Enhanced Vision Systems
item.description: Interested in EFVS displays (Enhanced Flight Vision Systems) and their use of an interface that is conformal to the scene around it - i.e. elements displayed are exactly in the right position relative to the real world. The interface is contextual, is aware of the pilot’s peripheral vision, does not limit the pilot to completely rely on it and only shows specific information in a dense space. It made me think how an interface such as this can be used in day to day activities.

item.title: some books I read in 2022 w/ notes
item.description: Zen Training - Katsuki Sekida Bought this book to study when I wanted to learn how to meditate properly. A simple and elegant style of writing explains mediation, breath work and posture clearly and eloquently. After practicing in between reading chapters of this book, I felt much more confident to meditate and would often practice in the park. A Nation in Pain - Judy Foreman Bought this book after recovering from a long term neck and shoulder pain.

item.title: Generated Worlds - an app concept
item.description: On the train from Lancaster to London I designed an app on Figma based on an art piece that I created two years ealier. This piece was called ‘Generated_worlds’ and was a python program that I wrote which essentially combined lots of different words randomly and put them together to create a new way of describing a world. For example the words: ‘Nano’ ‘Ecology’ and ‘Condition’ would create ‘Nano-ecology Condition’. Or ‘Relapse’ ‘Planetary’ and ‘Language’ would create ‘Relapse Planetary Language’.

item.title: life in sections
item.description: I divide up my life into a lot of sections. Big sections, small sections - weeks, days, months, eras, periods, bits, weekends, holidays, afternoons. Self structured sections - “this is a time when I need to focus, work a job, settle into a routine for a bit.” “These next few weeks I’m able to take a break - slow down and clear my head.’ Thinking back to the Summer of 2019, maybe it was 2018, I went to Montreal for a month and worked in a hostel then the plan was to get a bus to Boston to visit my cousins.

item.title: Late night manifesto for starfish.systems
item.description: OK so a few ideas are coming together and it’s starting to take a shape. The form it’s taking is as a tiny, experimental design studio which I’ve called ‘starfish.systems’ (named after a publication I designed a year ago). How I’m going to draw a frame around this form is through a brief manifesto that I wrote late at night quickly and without overthinking it. Here it goes - 1 - build components not complete designs

item.title: Where's the slime?
item.description: Thought experiment - what if visual designs, components and websites weren’t clean with gradients, fluid animations, pixel perfect serif fonts? What if they were dirty, yucky, grew within your viewport like fungus? Images and text decayed over time. Link rot was encouraged. Sections that were separate gradually meld together to create new forms. Not the wisest idea for client work but I might start researching if any one else is exploring this area.

item.title: Narrative as a design tool
item.description: Recently I have been interested in the possibility of narrative as a design tool/research process. My story, currently with no working title, is the ‘coming-into-life’ a number of ideas, relationships, beliefs and personal experiences of mine put into a semi-coherent narrative. With writing I have found that ideas that I once thought were very much separate were in fact deeply connected while others that I felt had more important were placed in the periphery.

item.title: 2Advanced Studios
item.description: The golden age of web design 2Advanced Studios v2 flash website in 2001#WebDesignHistory pic.twitter.com/HI9YbyVGIL — Web Design Museum (@WebDesignMuseum) September 22, 2022 I saw this post from the Web Design Museum captioned ‘The golden age of web design’. It made me think of the fusion between games and digital design, there’s an energy and a coherent form. The internet arrived and provided a path forwards, code provided the means for a new aesthetic.

item.title: On being ok with other people being at different stages in their lives than you
item.description: Much like how light can be a particle or a wave, other people’s life trajectories can be both parallel to yours and inhabit a completely different dimension. Since reading this article called ‘Instead of your life’s purpose’, I’ve been considering a new perspective of life being non-linear — life that doesn’t move in a straight line, is not scalable and is not an easily dividable experience. Accepting this perspective has eased a lot of rigidity that I’d been feeling, days flow a bit easier now and there’s more space for meaning to shine through.
contadt

item.title: 'Begin' Exhibition Poster'
item.description: Poster designed for a group painting exhibition at Ballymaloe Grainstore.

item.title: “Hell on earth”: satellite images document the siege of a Sudanese city
item.description: Built the graphics for this piece showing the war-related fires in Darfur as well as the destruction in el-Fasher and neighbouring villages and camps. Part of a series of articles and films from The Economist about the disaster taking place in Sudan. Collaboration with Yale Humanitarian Research Lab and the Centre for Information Resilience. Read the full piece here

item.title: Black Hole
item.description: Each dot = 50,000 data records collected by ‘Black Hole’ GCHQ program each day. (This includes: search history, social media posts and messages, call logs, emails and more) Coptic book binding by hand.

item.title: Election by Communication
item.description: drawingmachine.py Python program that draws based on live tweets on the 2016 US election depending on specific keywords. The bottom image depicts just a snapshot of the constant stream of content that was being used to draw these shapes.

item.title: Elevator pitch
item.description: Self-published zine on non-verbal communication and anxiety.

item.title: Enhanced Vision.OTF
item.description: Enhanced Vision is a typeface (Uppercase only) built from the components of Enhanced Flight Vision System screens. Download

item.title: Estimated building damage in Gaza
item.description: Mapping Israel’s war in Gaza building by building. Built for the Data Journalism team at The Economist.

item.title: Exhibitions/Residencies/Publications
item.description: Joya: Artist in Residence (2022) University College London - Multimedia Anthropology Lab collaborative exhibition (virtual) - uclmal.com/exhibition ‘Open Fragments’ - Pragamata Collective (online) ‘Gaps between gaps’ - Solo Exhibition Stallan-Brand Architects (Glasgow) ‘Fantôme/Aave’ - Vaapan Taiteen Tila (Helsinki)/Galerie au Medicis (Paris) ‘How to Survive Kaamos’ exhibition at Vapaan Taiteen Tila (Helsinki) ‘Rooted’ - Where People Sleep (Glasgow) ‘Sobremesa’ - Wasps Studios (Glasgow) ‘More is Less’ - Laurieston Arches (Glasgow) ‘Feedback Loop’ - GSA Photography studio

item.title: Fantôme/Aave
item.description: ‘This is Fiction’ - Single Channel Video Projection (First Image) In collaboration with Mensonge Collectif, co-curated with Cesar Kaci and Aliha Thalien Vapaan Taiteen Tila Helsinki/Galerie au Medicis Helsinki - 2019 Link to full video

item.title: Gaps between gaps
item.description: Stallan-Brand (Glasgow) March 12 - April 3 2020 An exhibition on conspiracy and world-building.

item.title: Generated glitch model paintings
item.description: Generated, Glitch, Model (GGM) Paintings - 3D Digital Render - Ongoing Experimenting with a visual medium developed using a variety of online AI text generators, picture editing phone apps and Maya software which I named Generated, Glitch, Model (GGM) paintings. blue systems, 2022 spacing, 2022

item.title: Generated Worlds
item.description: Python script that generates random terms for world building - making use of terms from politics, philosophy, ecology, computing, psychology and geography. Find the code here

item.title: Info
item.description: Visual journalist, Data wrangler, Artist and sometimes a Writer and Curator. My interests are in visual storytelling, data collection, visualisation and OSINT. Currently I’m working as an Interactive Visual Journalist at The Economist. Orbits - Trust UCL MAL The New Design Congress Campaign Lab g0v.london Reading The Signal and the Noise - Nate Silver Working on A film about undersea internet infrastructure ‘Pre-server’ - short sci-fi about lichen computer interfaces, planetary intelligence, and ghosts Recent work Detailed structural damage map of Gaza Strip for The Economist Visuals for a investigative piece on the Barclay brothers I also occasionally write some art criticism on my blog Generated Worlds

item.title: Keywords - Poster Series
item.description: A series of five posters on leaked keywords used in NSA Surveillance software.

item.title: Population Density Maps
item.description: Series of maps indicating population density in parts of America, Europe and Asia. Created using QGIS and edited using Pixelmator Pro.

item.title: Pretend Ai, Pretending to be People
item.description: Collaborative exhibition with UCL’s Multimedia Anthropology Lab (2021) This virtual space presents an ongoing conversation and collaboration between artists Zach Mason and Joseph Clark both who are interested in data collection, visualisation and the transformations of artificial intelligence. In a bedroom, inspired by the setting that many conversations between us have taken place, we will take on the role of AI where we limit ourselves to responding only to what has been said through the information present in the room, without the context of each others prior intentions or personal lives.

item.title: Savvy
item.description: Case Study Project - Savvy “We are Savvy, an online financial advice hub and community aimed at helping younger adults improve their lives and careers with financial literacy. Our Mission - Partner with a generation of young, independent and creative individuals to reach their full potential by improving their understanding of finance. So much financial advice can be out of date, dull and inaccessible to a younger generation - Savvy aims to change that.

item.title: Small boats API
item.description: I noticed that the UK government website keeps a live updated table of refugee small boat arrivals for the last 7 days. There wasn’t an API for this data and I thought it could use one. Using Val Town I created an API that fetches this data so it can be easily more easily accessed and immplemented into research projects. Link to API here

item.title: Spatial Finance
item.description: In the back of my mind for some time has been this article about Spatial Software and it got me thinking about some use cases. Most instances of spatial software seem to be communication or visual design related but after doing some data visualisation work I thought wouldn’t it be useful if trading/personal finance applications had an infinite canvas as well? This may be wrong but I started drawing up some mockups of what an application could look like.

item.title: Starfish
item.description: Self-published zine - 2021 Starfish Press began as a continuation to the publication of ‘Starfish’ in 2021. Still in the process of being established, the aim of Starfish is to create visual work which encounters new ideas that bridge print and digital culture. Co-editor and photographer - Annie Joy Boothroyd

item.title: starfish.systems - a tiny design studio
item.description: Case Study Project A tiny, experimental design studio activated by Joseph. starfish.systems is designed to run in digital spaces utilising a variety of tech and design stacks. Mood board and colour palette First step was to put together a moodboard which I had already partly accumulated on are.na. I was looking for retro, low poly shapes mixed with high tech ‘clean’ sci-fi tech. Based on some images I found I started building my colour palette.

item.title: The Economist US election forecast
item.description: This project represents the final results of our work to design and build the interactive visualisations for the Economist’s 2024 US election forecast. My part began by building out wireframes based on the 2016 election and 2020 midterms which served as the initial framework. We discussed what worked from those interactives and what needed to change and iterated on those in the design. It was important to find the right balance between clear topline information while giving the appropriate context in detail.

item.title: The secret deal that saved the Barclays
item.description: I designed a diagram for the Economist explaining the fraudulant financial transactions carried out by the Barclay brothers. Read the full investigation here

item.title: You Do You - Homeware
item.description: Speculative homeware brand - ‘You Do You’ Launch ‘This small independent homeware company is dedicated to creating high quality and sustainable products that reflect the tastes and styles of local creatives. Operating out of their retail shop in London, they believe in supporting local artisans and makers while promoting sustainability in their production methods. With a variety of items ranging from furniture to decor, all of their products are designed with a special focus on craftsmanship.
